## Update

Anthropic's [Circuit tracer](https://github.com/decoderesearch/circuit-tracer) works with a local installation on M1 with 16GB unified RAM for very small models. We also added additional rationale and critique to our [proposal](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1iEc51VGEFOR8M6djNYDpb-uQ3s_SKp4VFSJnAjti7to/edit?usp=drivesdk) to support a fundamental intuition for how Transformers work and how sparse autoencoders can help us interpret them. 

## Goal

The goal was to verify that the project installs correctly and lets us run a lightweight end-to-end example from the command line without getting blocked by bugs, very large model downloads, very slow model computation, etc so that we can begin to establish an automation pipeline for attribute inspection that enables rapid iteration over hypotheses.

We successfully got a Gemma-based circuit-tracer workflow running with google/gemma-3-270m, and documented the main steps, tradeoffs, and fixes along the way.  Install according to the README and run the following for a quick start. You must gain access to the appropriate model class on HuggingFace by making an account, then reading the terms of use for the given model class and accepting. You must create a huggingface token in your environment. 

In short, we:

1. Verified the package installation from github.  
2. Determined that Llama and Gemma 2 were both too heavyweight and approval-gated for a good first test.  
3. Switched focus to the smaller google/gemma-3-270m    
4. Clarified the sequence of model downloads and transcoder downloads.  
5. Confirmed how caching behaves across reruns.  
6. Generated a .pt graph file.  
7. Converted that graph into viewer JSON files.  
8. Successfully launched the local graph viewer for interactive Attribute inspection.

## Example output

An interactive Attribute graph for the prompt, "The capital of France is", demonstrating a top next token prediction of "Paris" along with feature (node) connectivity.  
![][image1]

## Why We Switched Away From Llama and Gemma 2

The initial attempt used:

circuit-tracer attribute \\  
  \--prompt "The capital of France is" \\  
  \--transcoder\_set llama \\  
  \--graph\_output\_path france\_capital.pt

These models  both turned out to be a poor smoke test for two reasons:

1. All commands trigger large transcoder downloads before attribution even starts. These models are large and slow for both Gemma2 and Llama.   
2. The Llama base model requires separate access approval that I did not originally have.  
3. The Llama and Gemma2 model themselves take up a lot of disk space.

We confirmed from the code that the order of downloads proceeds with transcoder then base model, eg llama is just a shortcut for the transcoder repo, while the base model is loaded afterward.

## Why We Focused On Small Gemma

Because the main bottlenecks were download size and computation speed, we switched our attention to a smaller Gemma model. Instead of staying on Gemma 2 2B, we used google/gemma-3-270m, which is much lighter and better suited to a first successful run when download size matters.

This required using an explicit Gemma 3 model name, a matching GemmaScope-2 transcoder, and installation of the nnsight backend.

The Gemma command that ultimately worked for generating graph files and starting the viewer was as follows. This command should generate the graph output and start the local server for inspection. 

circuit-tracer attribute \\  
  \--prompt "The capital of France is" \\  
  \--model "google/gemma-3-270m" \\  
  \--transcoder\_set "mwhanna/gemma-scope-2-270m-pt/clt/width\_262k\_l0\_medium\_affine" \\  
  \--backend nnsight \\  
  \--slug france-capital \\  
  \--graph\_file\_dir ./graph\_files \\  
  \--server

Alternatively the below command will generate the graph output in a .pt file. This file is a serialized PyTorch graph object, france\_capital.pt. You must convert it to make it viewable in the browser. 

circuit-tracer attribute \\  
  \--model "google/gemma-3-270m" \\  
  \--prompt "The capital of France is" \\  
  \--transcoder\_set "mwhanna/gemma-scope-2-270m-pt/clt/width\_262k\_l0\_medium\_affine" \\  
  \--graph\_output\_path france\_capital.pt \\  
  \--backend nnsight \\

## Circuit Tracer requires a Base model and Transcoder models

One recurring point of confusion was due to the two kinds of model artifacts circuit-tracer uses:

- the base language model  
- the transcoders used for attribution \- circuit tracer can only be used with models for which a transcoder has been trained by someone in the community otherwise you must train your own. 

These artifacts download with the "attribute" command. Confusingly, a run can appear to be "fetching again" even when only one of those artifacts still needs downloading. We determined that:

- the fast Fetching 26 files: 100% output likely represents cached transcoder resolution  
- the slower model-00001-of-00003.safetensors style output was the base model download

We also clarified that:

- Hugging Face cache stores the downloaded model files and raw downloaded artifacts  
- circuit-tracer has its own optional transcoder cache under \~/.cache/circuit\_tracer but in our case the hugging\_face directory stores all cached models including the transcoders.   
- interrupting a download should usually preserve already cached files, so reruns do not start completely from zero

## Converting The .pt File For Viewing

To view an attribute .pt file result in the local browser UI, we used create\_graph\_files(...) and then started the server.

python \-c 'from circuit\_tracer.utils.create\_graph\_files import create\_graph\_files; create\_graph\_files("france\_capital.pt", slug="france-capital", output\_path="./graph\_files")' && circuit-tracer start-server \--graph\_file\_dir ./graph\_files

## Viewing Results

Output shows that the local viewer starts on port 8041:

INFO: Starting server on port 8041...  
INFO: Serving data from: /Users/anthony/repos/amu/graph\_files  
INFO: Press Ctrl+C to stop the server.  
At that point, the graph can be viewed in a browser at:

- http://localhost:8041

## Recommended Next Steps

For the next iteration, keep using the same google/gemma-3-270m \+ nnsight workflow as the lightweight baseline, and only scale up to larger Gemma or Llama variants once the end-to-end process is stable.  


[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
